children.description: i’m following. the short answer is: it helps, but only in a limited way.

the pain you get from a loaded carry is mostly muscular tension: forearms, upper back, core, sometimes legs. it’s a slow, constant burn that teaches you to stay calm while something aches.

running pain is different. most of it comes from cardiovascular load, breathing rhythm, leg fatigue, and the “keep going even though your lungs want to stop” feeling. that’s a different physiological driver.

so the transfer looks like this:

what it does transfer:
• staying composed under steady discomfort
• not panicking when something burns
• the mental ability to hold a pace despite rising fatigue

what it doesn’t really transfer:
• cardio tolerance
• breathing control under effort
• dealing with impact and leg-heavy fatigue
• the specific rhythm of running discomfort

so it helps your mindset and your ability to endure discomfort, but it won’t replace actual running work. think of it as building the general “don’t quit when it hurts” muscle, not the “push through mile three” muscle.

if you want pain tolerance specifically for running, you’ll get the most benefit by pairing carries with short, controlled running intervals. if you want, i can lay out how to blend the two without overtaxing yourself.
